title: 🎙️ Cariboo Local Affairs
description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Cop fired and arrested after pulling gun on colleague who microwaved fish
items.content_html:
A South Carolina cop was so infuriated by another officer microwaving fish that he aimed his service firearm at him. Michael Debiase, 46, was fired, arrested, and charged with pointing and presenting a firearm at a person.

The South Carolina Law Enforcement Division investigated the incident, reports ABC News, which took place at Myrtle Beach Police Department. — Read the rest

The post Cop fired and arrested after pulling gun on colleague who microwaved fish appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] St Georges Guildhall in Norfolk, England
items.content_html:
St Georges Guildhall in King's Lynn, Norfolk , England was built around 1420 as a guildhall for a religious fellowship (not to be confused with merchant and craft  guilds which served a different  purpose) in King's Lynn. Religious guilds and the closely related chantries were dissolved by Edward  VI in 1547 at which time the guildhall became the property of Lynn Corporation (the dissolution was largely  motivated by the transfer assets from religious  institutions to the Crown). It is the largest surviving medieval guildhall in the country.

From the start of its history the building was used for theatrical performances, at first amateur ones but quickly by professional troupes of actors, the first being a nativity play in 1445 and initially the performances  were, mainly, religious plays. After the dissolution of the religious guilds the building was regularly used by a variety of theatrical companies and local tradition has it that William Shakespeare appeared here as an actor, as part of the troupe called the Earl of Pembroke's men in 1593 at a time when troupes of players were driven out of London by the plague. Shakespeare's best known comic actor Robert Armin was born one street away. A scene, in Hamlet, is widely said to have been inspired by an incident in King's Lynn.

Theatrical performances were curtailed when banned by the Commonwealth Government of Oliver Cromwell in the early 17th century but resumed after Charles II regained his throne in 1660. After a competing theatre was constructed in the town the guildhall was sold (1826) and for a short time was used as a warehouse. After a number of other uses, including a period of dereliction, the guildhall was restored by the architect Marshall Sisson and then given to the National Trust (1951). Subsequently it has been developed into an arts centre and theatre with the former warehouses in the courtyard were converted into art galleries as early as 1963.

Tree ring dating had shown that a floor exposed during renovations in 2023 dated from the time that Shakespeare is reputed to have performed here. In 2024  a door was exposed behind a plasterboard panel which was 600 years old  and is thought to have been the entrance to the dressing room used by Shakespeare.
items.summary: St Georges Guildhall in King's Lynn, Norfolk , England was built around 1420 as a guildhall for a religious fellowship (not to be confused with merchant and craft guilds which served a different purpose) in King's Lynn. Religious guilds and the closely related chantries were dissolved by Edward VI
